summary: Chilean Nobel Prize-winning poet, born Ricardo Reyes Basoalto, who took his pen name from Czech poet Jan Neruda. Known for Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Canto General (including 'The Heights of Macchu Picchu'), and the Elemental Odes. Also a diplomat and Communist. His work ranges from love poetry to political verse inspired by the Spanish Civil War and Latin American history. Always wrote in green ink.

works.name: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair
works.description: Neruda's early collection of love poems, published when he was 19. Translated by W. S. Merwin. Includes poems like 'Body of a Woman,' 'Tonight I Can Write the Saddest Lines,' 'White Bee,' 'In My Sky at Twilight,' 'Ah Vastness of Pines,' and the final 'Song of Despair.' Vicente Huidobro accused Neruda of plagiarizing Tagore's The Gardener in the poem 'In My Sky at Twilight.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Tonight I Can Write the Saddest Lines': 'Love is so short, forgetting is so long'; 'The night is starry and the stars are blue and shiver in the distance'
examples: The line 'Love is so short, forgetting is so long' appears in a poem that states, 'Tonight I can write the saddest lines'
the speaker uses 'The night is starry / and the stars are blue and shiver' as an example of the title phrases

clues.clue: 'The Song of Despair': refrains 'in you everything sank!' and 'it is the hour of departure'; 'The memory of you emerges from the night around me'; addressee is 'lost discoverer'
examples: the speaker proclaims 'it is the hour of departure' after lamenting 'in you everything sank!'
'The memory of you emerges from the night around me' starts a poem that also includes the repeated exclamation that 'In you everything sank!'

clues.clue: 'Body of a Woman': 'white hills, white thighs'; 'Body of a woman, white hills, white thighs, you look like a world, lying in surrender'
examples: A line about 'white hills, white thighs' opens this author's poem 'Body of a Woman'
opens with a poem celebrating the 'white hills, white thighs' of the title 'Body of a Woman'

clues.clue: 'Ah Vastness of Pines': beloved described as 'toy doll,' 'shell in whom the earth sings,' 'arms of transparent stone'; ends with 'ears of wheat toiling in the mouth of the wind'

works.name: Canto General / The Heights of Macchu Picchu
works.description: Neruda's magnum opus, a vast poetic sequence about the Americas. Sections include 'A Lamp on Earth,' 'The Heights of Macchu Picchu,' 'The Conquistadors,' 'The Sand Betrayed,' 'The Fugitive,' 'America, I Do Not Invoke Your Name in Vain,' and 'The Earth's Name is Juan.' 'The Heights of Macchu Picchu' is a 12-part poem about climbing to the Incan ruin, asking the builders to 'Rise up in birth with me, my brother.' Also includes poems attacking the United Fruit Company and 'Celestial Poets.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Arise to birth with me, my brother' / 'Speak through my words and my blood' / 'Fasten your bodies to me like magnets' — the Heights of Macchu Picchu addresses the builders of the ruin
examples: commands 'Arise to birth with me, my brother' in a section of a long poem
asks 'Give me silence, water, hope... Attach your bodies to me like magnets... Speak through my words and my blood'

clues.clue: The poem begins 'From breeze to breeze, like an empty net'; speaker climbs 'up the ladder of the earth' to the Incan ruin
examples: A 12-part poem begins 'From breeze to breeze, like an empty net'
this writer claims to have risen 'up the ladder of the earth' to an Incan ruin

clues.clue: Sections include 'A Lamp on Earth,' 'The Conquistadors,' 'The Sand Betrayed,' 'The Fugitive,' 'America, I Do Not Invoke Your Name in Vain'
examples: That poem comes between 'A Lamp on Earth' and 'The Conquistadors' in a larger work

clues.clue: Opens with 'Before the wig and the dress coat / there were rivers, arterial rivers'; 'The Birds Arrive' follows 'Some Beasts'

works.name: Elemental Odes
works.description: A collection of odes addressed to everyday objects and foods. Includes odes to socks (gift from Maru Mori), a large tuna in the market, an artichoke, tomatoes, conger chowder, salt, maize, a watermelon ('green whale of summer'), a seashore cactus, and an atom. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Ode to socks: gift knitted by Maru Mori; 'beauty is twice beauty and what is good is doubly good'; socks 'as soft as rabbits'; feet look like 'two long sharks' or 'two immense blackbirds'; impulse to put them in a 'golden cage'
examples: The line 'beauty is twice beauty' appears in one of these poems about a gift knitted by Maru Mori
These objects, which are 'as soft as rabbits,' make part of the speaker's body seem like 'two long sharks'

clues.clue: Ode to a large tuna in the market: 'bullet from the ocean depths'; 'among the market greens'; tuna described as 'catafalqued king'; 'navigating the waters of death'
examples: addressed to 'a bullet / from the ocean / depths' lying 'among the market greens'
describes a large tuna 'navigating the waters of death'

clues.clue: Ode to artichoke: 'dressed up like a warrior' before being stripped 'scale by scale' to eat 'the peaceful mush of its green heart'
examples: The title object is 'dressed up like a warrior' before it is stripped 'scale by scale' to eat the 'peaceful mush of its green heart'

clues.clue: Ode to conger chowder: 'add the eel that it may be immersed in glory'; 'drop a dollop of cream into the concoction'
examples: the speaker prescribes, 'you add the eel / that it may be immersed in glory' and then states, 'Now all that remains is to / drop a dollop of cream / into the concoction'

clues.clue: Ode to tomatoes: people 'pour oil' onto 'halved hemispheres'; calls tomato 'star of earth'
examples: people 'pour / oil' onto the 'halved hemispheres' of a food it calls 'star of earth'

clues.clue: Ode to an atom: describes 'crazy spark' and 'smallest star'; 'We wake up' then 'the dawn had consumed itself. All the birds fell calcified'
examples: A 'crazy spark' and 'smallest star' are described in one of these poems about an atom

clues.clue: Ode to salt: 'you won't believe me but this thing sings'
examples: Neruda's ode to this substance proclaims 'You won't believe me but this thing sings'

clues.clue: Ode to maize: 'let history rest in its shroud'; 'America grew from' this

clues.clue: Ode to a watermelon: calls it the 'green whale of summer'

works.name: Walking Around / I Explain a Few Things / Residence on Earth
works.description: Residence on Earth is a collection written during Neruda's time as a diplomat. 'Walking Around' opens with 'It so happens I am sick of being a man' / 'I happen to be tired of being a man' and describes 'hideous intestines hanging over the doors of houses.' 'I Explain a Few Things' ends with 'Come and see the blood in the streets!' and was inspired by the Spanish Civil War in Madrid. Federico Garcia Lorca drew images to accompany 'Walking Around.' Also includes 'Alberto Rojas Jimenez Comes Flying.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: 'Walking Around': 'It so happens I am sick of being a man'; imagined himself as 'a felt swan navigating in water of sources and ashes'; encounters 'hideous intestines hanging over doors'; Lorca drew images to accompany it
examples: opens with the proclamation, 'I happen to be tired of being a man'
This poet imagined himself as 'withered, impenetrable, like a felt swan navigating in the water of sources and ashes'

clues.clue: 'I Explain a Few Things': ends 'Come and see the blood in the streets!'; inspired by Spanish Civil War in Madrid; set 'with bells, and clocks, and trees'
examples: repeated the command, 'Come and see the blood / in the streets!' at the end of his poem 'I Explain a Few Things'
inspired by his time living in Madrid during the Spanish Civil War

clues.clue: Residence on Earth written during diplomatic postings; includes Spain in Our Hearts
examples: This poet's collections Spain in Our Hearts and Residence on Earth draw on his time as a diplomat in Europe
wrote the first section of his collection Residence on Earth while stationed as a diplomat away from his native Chile

clues.clue: 'Alberto Rojas Jimenez Comes Flying': about a drowned poet who 'comes flying' 'over dentists and parishes / cinemas, tunnels, and ears'
examples: Alberto Rojas, a drowned poet who 'comes flying' in a poem from Residence on Earth

works.name: One Hundred Love Sonnets
works.description: A collection of 100 love sonnets, many likely about Matilde Urrutia. Sonnet XVII begins 'I don't love you as if you were a rose of salt, topaz' and includes 'I love you as one loves certain obscure things, / secretly, between the shadow and the soul.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Sonnet XVII: 'I don't love you as if you were a rose of salt, topaz'; 'I love you as one loves certain obscure things, secretly, between the shadow and the soul'; 'so close that your hand upon my chest is mine'

examples: Neruda's love sonnet 16 begins 'I don't love you as if you were a rose of this thing, topaz, or arrow of carnations'

works.name: Other Poems and Works
works.description: The Captain's Verses (includes 'Your Laughter' and 'If You Forget Me'; dedicated to future wife Matilde Urrutia). 'If You Forget Me': 'if suddenly you forget me / do not look for me / for I already have forgotten you.' Los versos del capitan published anonymously. Also wrote Confieso que he vivido (memoirs) and Grapes and the Wind (contains pro-Stalin sections). Mourned Cesar Vallejo in an ode. Ode to Jean Arthur Rimbaud. Isla Negra is frequently referenced as his home.

clues.clue: 'If You Forget Me': 'if suddenly you forget me / do not look for me / for I already have forgotten you'; 'everything that exists, aromas, light, metals, are little boats that sail toward the isles of the addressee'
examples: tells the addressee that if the title event occurs, 'do not look for me, for I shall already have forgotten you'
'everything that exists, aromas, light, metals,' are 'little boats that sail toward' the 'isles' of the addressee

clues.clue: 'Your Laughter': 'deny me bread, air, light, spring / but do not take from me your laughter'; from The Captain's Verses

included the poem 'Your Laughter' in his collection The Captain's Verses

comprehensive_summary:
Pablo Neruda was a Chilean Nobel Prize-winning poet, born Ricardo Reyes Basoalto, who took his pen name from Czech poet Jan Neruda. He always wrote in green ink, which he considered the color of hope. A Communist who served as a senator, he was also a diplomat with consulships throughout Asia and Spain.

His most frequently clued collection is Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, published when he was 19 and translated by W. S. Merwin. It includes 'Tonight I Can Write the Saddest Lines' ('Love is so short, forgetting is so long'; 'The night is starry and the stars are blue and shiver in the distance'), 'Body of a Woman' ('white hills, white thighs'), 'White Bee,' 'Ah Vastness of Pines,' and the final 'Song of Despair' (refrains 'in you everything sank!' and 'it is the hour of departure'; opens 'The memory of you emerges from the night around me'). Vicente Huidobro accused Neruda of plagiarizing Tagore's The Gardener for the poem 'In My Sky at Twilight.'

Canto General is his magnum opus, a vast poetic sequence about the Americas. It includes 'The Heights of Macchu Picchu,' a 12-part poem that begins 'From breeze to breeze, like an empty net' and commands 'Arise to birth with me, my brother' and 'Speak through my words and my blood.' Sections include 'A Lamp on Earth,' 'The Conquistadors,' 'The Sand Betrayed,' 'The Fugitive,' and 'America, I Do Not Invoke Your Name in Vain.' The collection also includes 'The Invisible Man' ('I laugh / I smile / at the old poets'), poems attacking the United Fruit Company and 'Celestial Poets,' and 'The Earth's Name is Juan.' Neruda's election as senator overlapped with completing 'The Heights of Machu Picchu.'

The Elemental Odes are addressed to everyday objects: socks (knitted by Maru Mori; 'beauty is twice beauty'; socks 'as soft as rabbits'; feet look like 'two long sharks'), a large tuna ('bullet from the ocean depths'; 'catafalqued king'), an artichoke ('dressed up like a warrior'), tomatoes ('star of earth'), conger chowder, salt ('you won't believe me but this thing sings'), maize ('America grew from' it), a watermelon ('green whale of summer'), a seashore cactus, and an atom ('crazy spark').

'Walking Around' opens with 'It so happens I am sick of being a man' and describes 'hideous intestines hanging over doors'; Federico Garcia Lorca drew images to accompany it. 'I Explain a Few Things' ends 'Come and see the blood in the streets!' and was inspired by the Spanish Civil War. Both appear in Residence on Earth, written during his diplomatic postings.

One Hundred Love Sonnets includes Sonnet XVII ('I don't love you as if you were a rose of salt, topaz'; 'I love you as one loves certain obscure things, secretly, between the shadow and the soul'). The Captain's Verses includes 'Your Laughter' and 'If You Forget Me,' likely about his wife Matilde Urrutia. He anonymously published Los versos del capitan.

Neruda appears as a character in Antonio Skarmeta's The Postman / Burning Patience, where he provides advice to Mario on Isla Negra, and in Roberto Bolano's By Night in Chile. In his Nobel lecture, he rejected Huidobro's creationist slogan 'the poet is a little god' and quoted Rimbaud about conquering 'the splendid city' through 'burning patience.' He was criticized for failing to condemn the USSR forcing Pasternak to reject the 1958 Nobel Prize, and the Congress for Cultural Freedom tried to discredit him by publicizing pro-Stalin sections of Grapes and the Wind. Gabriela Mistral read his poems after moving to his hometown of Temuco.
